title: Cheddar and chive biscuits
subtitle: Savoury drop scones with mature cheddar
excerpt: Savoury drop biscuits packed with mature cheddar and fresh chives, brought together with cold milk in under 30 minutes. Serve warm alongside soup or split and spread with butter. Makes twelve.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: cheese scones are recorded as a savoury variation on the plain scone, made with flour, butter, milk, and grated hard cheese. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): cheese and herb scones appear in the regional savoury baking chapter, with chives cited as the classic British herb partner for cheddar.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled in a bag for up to 2 months. Reheat at 180°C for 8 minutes from frozen.
recipe.batchNotes: Double and bake on two trays.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Mix the dry ingredients and grate the cheese the night before; chill separately.

content.text: These are drop biscuits rather than rolled-and-cut scones, which means the batter is wetter and the edges come out rough and craggy rather than clean. The cheddar melts into pockets through the crumb; use a mature one for a flavour that survives the bake. Chives can be swapped for spring onion tops, but use twice the volume.

Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Whisk the {{self-raising-flour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a large bowl. Rub in the {{unsalted-butter}} until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs with some butter pieces still visible. Stir through most of the {{cheddar}} (reserving 20 g) and all the {{chives}}. Pour in the {{whole-milk}} and stir briefly with a fork until a sticky dough comes together; do not overmix. Drop large spoonfuls of about 60 g each onto the lined tray, spacing them 4 cm apart. Scatter the reserved {{cheddar}} over the tops. Bake for 12–15 minutes until the tops are deep gold and the cheese is bubbling at the edges. Cool on a rack for 5 minutes before serving.
